input.old_string: - The `eyedropper_supported` context field is set via runtime detection (`"EyeDropper" in window`) during the `detect-eyedropper` effect when the picker opens.
input.new_string: - The `eyedropper_supported` context field starts `false`. When the picker opens, the machine emits the `Effect::DetectEyedropper` intent; the adapter performs the `"EyeDropper" in window` runtime detection (the core never touches `window`) and reports the result back via `Event::SetEyedropperSupported(bool)`.

content.content: The file /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300/spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.oldString: - The `eyedropper_supported` context field is set via runtime detection (`"EyeDropper" in window`) during the `detect-eyedropper` effect when the picker opens.
toolUseResult.newString: - The `eyedropper_supported` context field starts `false`. When the picker opens, the machine emits the `Effect::DetectEyedropper` intent; the adapter performs the `"EyeDropper" in window` runtime detection (the core never touches `window`) and reports the result back via `Event::SetEyedropperSupported(bool)`.

lines: -- The `eyedropper_supported` context field is set via runtime detection (`"EyeDropper" in window`) during the `detect-eyedropper` effect when the picker opens.

+- The `eyedropper_supported` context field starts `false`. When the picker opens, the machine emits the `Effect::DetectEyedropper` intent; the adapter performs the `"EyeDropper" in window` runtime detection (the core never touches `window`) and reports the result back via `Event::SetEyedropperSupported(bool)`.
- When `eyedropper_supported` is `false`, the `EyeDropperTrigger` part is hidden (`hidden` attribute set).
- `Event::EyedropperRequest` is only processed when `eyedropper_supported` is `true` and the component is not `readonly`.
- The adapter calls `EyeDropper.open()` directly from a click event handler (within user gesture, not from an async callback) to satisfy the transient activation requirement.

content.text: Now realign the §6.1 parity table after the wider `swatches` row.

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
python3 - <<'PY'
path="spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md"
lines=open(path).read().split("\n")
# Find the §6.1 props parity table header: starts with "| Feature " 
start=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if l.startswith("| Feature ") and "ars-ui" in l)
end=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines[start:], start) if l.strip()=="")
rows=[]
for l in lines[start:end]:
    if set(l.strip())<=set("|-: "):
        rows.append(None); continue
    rows.append([c.strip() for c in l.strip().strip("|").split("|")])
ncol=len(next(r for r in rows if r))
widths=[0]*ncol
for r in rows:
    if r:
        for i,c in enumerate(r): widths[i]=max(widths[i],len(c))
def fmt(r): return "| "+" | ".join(c.ljust(widths[i]) for i,c in enumerate(r))+" |"
def sep(): return "| "+" | ".join("-"*widths[i] for i in range(ncol))+" |"
out=[sep() if r is None else fmt(r) for r in rows]
lines[start:end]=out
open(path,"w").write("\n".join(lines))
print("realigned §6.1 table", start+1, "to", end)
PY

//! per-channel text inputs, a hex input, runtime format switching
//! (hex/rgb/hsl/hsb), runtime color-space switching, preset swatches, and an
-//! optional browser EyeDropper integration. The agnostic core owns the
+//! optional browser `EyeDropper` integration. The agnostic core owns the
//! composite color value, the channel math, the popover open/drag lifecycle,
//! containment, focus restoration, and the EyeDropper browser API all belong to
//! the framework adapter. The adapter supplies already-normalized `(x, y)` in
-//! browser EyeDropper support back via [`Event::SetEyedropperSupported`], and
//! resolves the typed [`Effect`] intents emitted on the open/close lifecycle.
//! The string IDs in [`Context`] exist purely for ARIA wiring and
//! hydration-stable `id` attributes, never as a substitute for live handles.

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = [
  "ars-collections/std",
  "ars-core/std",
  "ars-forms/std",
  "ars-i18n/std",
  "ars-interactions/std",
  "idna/std",
]
debug = ["dep:log", "ars-core/debug", "ars-interactions/debug"]
# Enables ICU4X-backed locale-aware Unicode case mapping (via
# `ars-i18n/icu4x`) and unlocks components whose spec contract requires it
# — currently `utility::highlight`. Adapters that want the browser
# `Intl` backend instead can build with `--no-default-features --features
# std,ars-i18n/web-intl`; in that combination, modules gated on `i18n`
# (Highlight) are not available.
i18n = ["ars-collections/i18n", "ars-i18n/icu4x"]
uuid = ["ars-collections/uuid"]

content.text: The native coverage gate FAILED: `ars-components` branch coverage is **89.9%** vs the 90% minimum (lines 98.4% pass). My color_picker code added branches that aren't all exercised, nudging the crate 0.1% under. Let me find the uncovered *branches* (not just lines) in color_picker.

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
cargo llvm-cov test -p ars-components --branch --lib --text -- specialized::color_picker 2>&1 > /tmp/cp_branch.txt
python3 - <<'PY'
import re
lines=open("/tmp/cp_branch.txt").read().split("\n")
start=next((i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if "specialized/color_picker/mod.rs" in l and l.strip().endswith(":")), None)
if start is None:
    print("section not found"); raise SystemExit
prev=None
hits=[]
for l in lines[start+1:]:
    if re.match(r'^/.*\.rs:$', l.strip()): break
    # branch annotation lines look like:  "  |  Branch (LINE:COL): [True: 0, False: N]"
    if "Branch (" in l and ("True: 0" in l or "False: 0" in l):
        hits.append(l.strip())
print("uncovered branch annotations:", len(hits))
for h in hits[:60]: print(h)
PY

content.text: `--branch` needs nightly, but the gate parses branch data from the lcov it already generated (`target/coverage/native-fast.lcov`). Let me parse that lcov for color_picker's uncovered branch records.

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
ls -la target/coverage/native-fast.lcov 2>/dev/null && python3 - <<'PY'
path="target/coverage/native-fast.lcov"
cur=None; uncovered={}; totals={}
import collections
brda=collections.defaultdict(lambda:[0,0])  # file -> [taken, total]
for line in open(path):
    line=line.rstrip("\n")
    if line.startswith("SF:"):
        cur=line[3:]
    elif line.startswith("BRDA:") and cur:
        # BRDA:line,block,branch,taken  (taken '-' means not executed)
        parts=line[5:].split(",")
        ln=parts[0]; taken=parts[3]
        if "color_picker/mod.rs" in cur:
            t=brda[cur]
            t[1]+=1
            if taken!="-" and taken!="0":
                t[0]+=1
            else:
                uncovered.setdefault(cur,[]).append(ln)
for f,t in brda.items():
    print(f"{f}\n  branches: {t[0]}/{t[1]} = {100*t[0]/t[1]:.1f}%")
    u=uncovered.get(f,[])
    from collections import Counter
    c=Counter(u)
    print("  uncovered branch lines:", dict(c))
PY

content.text:
Implementation and the full post-implementation audit are complete. Here is the result for your review.

## Task #300 — ColorPicker agnostic core: ready for review

**What landed** (no commit yet — awaiting your approval):

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs` | **New** — full machine (`State`/`Event`/`Effect`/`Context`/`Props`/`Messages`/`Machine`/`Part`/`Api`) + 79 unit tests + 26 committed `.snap` fixtures + module doctest |
| `crates/ars-components/src/specialized/mod.rs` | registered `pub mod color_picker` |
| `crates/ars-core/src/color.rs` | `ColorChannel` gains `Hash` (needed for `Part::ChannelSlider { channel }`) |
| `crates/ars-core/src/message_fn.rs` | two additive `From` impls for `&ColorValue` message closures |
| `crates/ars-components/tests/spec_conformance/specialized.rs` | `color_picker_anatomy_matches_spec` (all 17 parts) |
| `crates/ars-components/tests/proptest_state_machines/specialized.rs` | `color_picker_proptests` invariant module |
| `spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md` | §1.2–1.8 + §4.1/§5.1/§6.1 synced to the canonical contract |

**Key design decisions** (per your answers): typed `Effect` enum + adapter-wired DOM (matches popover/dialog); `Event::SetEyedropperSupported(bool)` for adapter-driven detection; adapter-driven drag (normalized coords, like the 7 siblings); `Part::Swatch { index }` resolving color from a new `swatches` prop (since `ColorValue` can't be `Eq`/`Hash`).

**Audit outcome (3 phases, all findings landed in this PR):**
- *Phase 1 (spec drift):* rewrote the prototype-style spec §1 to the canonical shape; `Messages: PartialEq`, `ColorChannel: Hash`, anatomy/§5.1/§6.1 corrections.
- *Phase 2 (3 rounds):* §6.1 `swatches` parity row; **10 clippy findings** fixed (zero-warning); markdown lint fixes; adapter specs/catalog/feature wiring verified consistent.
- *Phase 3 (coverage):* drove branch coverage up by adding targeted tests; one defensive div-by-zero guard left uncovered (unreachable for real channels).

**Verification (all green):**
- `cargo test -p ars-components --features i18n --all-targets` → 4541 lib + 114 conformance + proptests pass
- `cargo clippy -p ars-components -p ars-core --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` → clean
- `cargo insta test … --unreferenced=reject` → no orphan snapshots
- `cargo xtask spec validate` (340 files) + snippet-compile gate → pass
- `cargo xcov` native coverage gate → **PASS** (`ars-components` 98.5% line / 90.1% branch)

Issue #300 is **In Progress** on the board. Nothing has been committed or pushed.

Shall I proceed to run `cargo xci-fast` and then commit + open a PR to `main` (`Closes #300`, with the `snapshot-reviewed` label and the Codex-review loop)?

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

Always invoke with Skill() tool:

- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

Implement ColorPicker agnostic core (#300)

Add the framework-agnostic `ColorPicker` complex orchestrator: composite
color state, popover open/close + drag lifecycle, a 2D saturation/lightness
area, hue/alpha channel sliders, per-channel and hex text inputs, runtime
format and color-space switching, preset swatches, adapter-wired EyeDropper
integration, a form HiddenInput, and full ARIA / data-ars-* output.

The machine follows the canonical sibling conventions (typed Effect enum,
Bindable controlled/uncontrolled value+open, on_props_changed -> SyncValue /
SetProps, initial_effects mirroring the open lifecycle). Drag is adapter-driven
with normalized coordinates; EyeDropper support is reported back via
Event::SetEyedropperSupported; the core never touches the DOM or browser APIs.

Part::Swatch carries only an index (ColorValue is not Eq/Hash) and resolves its
color from a new `swatches` preset prop, matching color_swatch_picker.

Supporting ars-core additions: derive Hash on ColorChannel (so it can
parameterize a ComponentPart variant) and two &ColorValue MessageFn From impls
for the swatch/color-name/announcement messages.

Tests: 79 unit + 26 committed snapshot fixtures, a spec-conformance anatomy
test over all 17 parts, a proptest invariant module, and a module doctest.

The color-picker spec section 1 was authored in an older prototype style
(string-named effects, undefined helpers, an `id: ComponentId` that never
existed, no typed Machine::Effect); it is rewritten to the implemented contract
in the same change per the spec-sync rule.

Closes #300

cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
gh pr create --base main --head impl-300 --title "Implement ColorPicker agnostic core (#300)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Implements the framework-agnostic **ColorPicker** complex orchestrator (issue #300, epic #227): composite color state, popover open/close + drag lifecycle, a 2D saturation/lightness area, hue/alpha channel sliders, per-channel and hex text inputs, runtime format and color-space switching, preset swatches, adapter-wired EyeDropper integration, a form `HiddenInput`, and full ARIA / `data-ars-*` output. Framework adapters (Leptos/Dioxus) are out of scope.

## Design

- **Typed `Effect` enum** (`ChangeEnd`, `AttachClickOutside`/`DetachClickOutside`, `DetectEyedropper`, `InvokeEyedropper`, `AnnounceColorSpace`) — the agnostic core never touches the DOM or browser APIs; adapters resolve the intents.
- **`Bindable`** controlled/uncontrolled `value` and `open`; `on_props_changed` → `Open`/`Close` (controlled open), `SyncValue`, `SetProps`; `initial_effects` mirrors the open lifecycle for a booted-open mount.
- **Drag is adapter-driven** — the adapter measures the surface and sends normalized `(x, y)` via `DragStart`/`DragMove`/`DragEnd`, matching the 7 sibling color components (no element measurement inside core effects).
- **EyeDropper support** is reported back via `Event::SetEyedropperSupported(bool)` (the Chromium-only API has no `PlatformEffects` method).
- **`Part::Swatch { index }`** carries only an index (`ColorValue` is not `Eq`/`Hash`, which `ComponentPart` requires) and resolves its color from a new `swatches` preset prop — matching `color_swatch_picker::Part::Item`.

### Supporting `ars-core` additions
- `ColorChannel` derives `Hash` (needed to parameterize a `ComponentPart` variant).
- Two additive `&ColorValue` `MessageFn` `From` impls for the swatch / color-name / announcement messages.

## Spec sync

The color-picker spec §1 was authored in an early prototype style (string-named effects, undefined helpers, an `id: ComponentId` type that never existed, no typed `Machine::Effect`) that could not compile against the current `Machine` trait. It is rewritten to the implemented contract in this PR (§1.2–1.8, §4.1, §5.1, §6.1) per the spec-sync rule.

## Tests
- 79 unit tests + 26 committed `.snap` snapshot fixtures (every anatomy part + each output-affecting branch)
- spec-conformance anatomy test over all 17 parts
- proptest invariant module (`color_picker_proptests`)
- module-level doctest

## Verification
- `cargo xci --profile fast` — all 10 gates pass (fmt, clippy, unit, integration, adapter, spec-compile-snippets, error-variant-coverage, mutual-exclusion, snapshot-count, coverage-native)
- `ars-components` coverage 98.5% line / 90.1% branch (PASS); `cargo insta test --unreferenced=reject` clean; `cargo xtask spec validate` passes

A three-phase `post-implementation-audit` was run; all findings landed in this PR.

Closes #300

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300/.claude/skills/waiting-for-codex-review

# Waiting for Codex Review

Codex (the OpenAI Codex automated reviewer) reviews open PRs asynchronously. The reaction contract on the PR itself:

- **Run starts** → Codex adds 👀 (eyes). This is the "I'm looking" signal.
- **Run finishes — approved** → Codex *removes* 👀 and adds 👍 (thumbsup). No further action required.
- **Run finishes — changes requested** → Codex *removes* 👀 and posts one or more inline review threads. **No 👍 is added.** The disappearance of 👀 is the signal that this run finished with findings.
- **You push a fix** → Codex re-adds 👀 to start the next pass. The loop resumes.

The **👀-disappears** transition is load-bearing: it's how you distinguish "still reviewing" from "review done — go look at the threads". Don't keep waiting for 👍 once 👀 has dropped; switch to the thread-handling subroutine immediately.

This skill is the polling + response loop for that contract. You stay in it until 👍 lands.

## When to invoke

Invoke as soon as a PR is pushed and Codex is set up for the repository. Common trigger phrases:

- "wait for the codex review"
- "what does codex say about the PR"
- "address the codex feedback"
- "check the PR reviews"
- "is codex still reviewing"

If the user pushes a PR and gives no explicit next instruction, default to invoking this skill — the right next step is to watch for Codex.

## Reaction lookups: the exact data

PRs are issues for the reactions endpoint. The bot's login depends on the install — on installs that use the ChatGPT Codex connector it's `chatgpt-codex-connector`; on the standalone Codex Cloud install it may be `codex[bot]` or similar. The first poll of this loop **discovers the bot login** rather than hard-coding it: any review-comment author whose handle contains `codex` is treated as Codex.

```bash
# Reactions on the PR itself
gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions
# → array of { user.login, content } where content ∈ {'+1','-1','laugh','confused','heart','hooray','rocket','eyes'}
# +1 == 👍, eyes == 👀

# Reviews and review threads
gh pr view <pr> --json reviews
gh api graphql -f query='
  query($owner:String!,$repo:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$owner,name:$repo){
      pullRequest(number:$n){
        reviewThreads(first:100){
          nodes{
            id            # GraphQL node id — use this to RESOLVE
            isResolved
            comments(first:100){
              nodes{ databaseId author{login} path line body }
              # databaseId is the REST id — use this to REPLY
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }' -F owner=<owner> -F repo=<repo> -F n=<pr>
```

Keep two ids per thread straight: **`thread.id`** (GraphQL node id, opaque string starting with `PRRT_...`) is needed to *resolve* the thread; **`comment.databaseId`** (integer) is needed to *reply* in-line. They are not interchangeable.

## The loop

```dot
digraph codex_loop {
    poll [label="Poll reactions + threads", shape=diamond];
    thumbs [label="Codex 👍 on PR?", shape=diamond];
    eyes [label="Codex 👀 on PR?", shape=diamond];
    threads [label="Unresolved Codex threads?", shape=diamond];
    address [label="Address each thread (TDD)", shape=box];
    push [label="Commit + push fix", shape=box];
    reply [label="Reply inline + resolve thread", shape=box];
    trigger [label="Comment '@codex review'", shape=box];
    wait_review [label="Sleep — Codex still reviewing", shape=box];
    wait_start [label="Sleep — Codex hasn't started", shape=box];
    done [label="Done — hand back to user", shape=doublecircle];

    poll -> thumbs;
    thumbs -> done [label="yes"];
    thumbs -> eyes [label="no"];
    eyes -> wait_review [label="yes (still reviewing)"];
    eyes -> threads [label="no (👀 dropped → run finished)"];
    threads -> address [label="yes"];
    threads -> wait_start [label="no (pre-review, no run yet)"];
    wait_review -> poll;
    wait_start -> poll;
    address -> push -> reply -> trigger -> poll;
}
```

**Three terminal poll states matter:**

| State | What it means | What to do |
|---|---|---|
| 👍 present (👀 absent) | Codex approved | Done — hand back to user |
| 👀 present | Codex is actively reviewing right now | Sleep, re-poll |
| 👀 absent AND 👍 absent | Either Codex finished with findings, or it never started | Check threads — if any unresolved Codex thread exists, address them; otherwise treat as "not started yet" and sleep |

## Step-by-step

### 1. Identify the PR

If the user names the PR, use that number. Otherwise, fall back to the head branch:

```bash
gh pr view --json number,url,headRefName
```

Confirm the PR is open and was pushed in this session before entering the loop — don't enter the loop on a stale branch.

### 2. Kick off the initial review with `@codex review`

Codex does not always start a review automatically when a PR is opened. Some installs only trigger on explicit mention, others trigger automatically *most* of the time but not always. Either way, posting `@codex review` as a top-level comment right after the first push is the deterministic way to start the first review — and it's an idempotent no-op when Codex would have triggered on its own (Codex just sees the redundant mention and starts).

```bash
gh pr comment <pr> --body "@codex review"
```

Post this **once** immediately after entering the loop on a freshly-pushed PR, **before** the first snapshot/poll. This is the same trigger used in step 7 for subsequent passes — the difference is just timing (entry vs. after a fix push). If you entered the loop on a PR that already has a 👀 or 👍 reaction from Codex (e.g., the user invoked the skill mid-stream), skip this step — the review is already in flight.

### 3. Snapshot the current state

Before polling, capture the state so the next poll can detect *new* signals (not just any signals):

```text
seen_reactions := { (user, content) ... } from /issues/<pr>/reactions
seen_threads   := { thread.id ... } from the GraphQL query
```

### 4. Poll, decide, act

Read the **reaction set first**, threads second — the reaction is the run-state signal, the threads are the run *output*.

| 👀 on PR | 👍 on PR | Unresolved Codex threads | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| no | yes | (don't care) | Loop is done. Announce. |
| yes | no | (don't care) | Codex is reviewing right now. Sleep, then re-poll. |
| no | no | yes | Codex finished this run with findings — **the 👀 dropped, no 👍 was added**. Drop into the "address comments" subroutine. |
| no | no | no | Either Codex hasn't started yet (first poll after push), or every thread is already resolved and a fresh review is pending. Sleep, then re-poll. |

Decision-making notes:

- **The 👀 → ∅ transition is the "run finished with findings" signal.** Don't keep waiting for 👍 — switch to thread handling as soon as 👀 drops and threads exist.
- **Codex sometimes posts a top-level review body** ("LGTM" / a short summary) without 👍. Treat that as advisory — only the 👍 reaction is the terminal approval signal.
- **A new push does NOT reliably retrigger Codex.** The auto-retrigger fires sometimes but not always; do not rely on it. After every fix push + thread resolution, post `@codex review` as a fresh top-level comment to deterministically start the next pass (see step 7 of the response workflow). If 👀 does not reappear within ~3 minutes after the comment, double-check that the push actually landed on the PR branch (`gh pr view <pr> --json commits`).
- **Multiple Codex passes are normal.** The loop must tolerate Codex finding new issues introduced by your fix. Each pass is a fresh 👀 → (👍 | ∅ + threads) cycle, kicked off by a fresh `@codex review` comment.

### 5. Address review comments (the hard part)

Each unresolved Codex thread is a finding that must land a fix in *this* PR — no follow-up issues, no deferral. The bar is the same as the `post-implementation-audit` skill (read it if you haven't):

1. **Read the comment twice** before you start. Then read the surrounding 30 lines of the file. Codex's comments are usually right but sometimes context-thin — your job is to understand whether the suggested change is the *best outcome* or whether the underlying concern can be fixed differently. If you disagree, see "Disagreeing with Codex" below.
2. **Write the test first.** For every code change you make, add or update a named test that *fails on the current code* and *passes after the fix*. Skipping the test is what lets the regression come back silently on the next iteration. This applies even to "obvious" fixes — obvious code regresses too.
3. **Coverage must not regress.** Capture the current per-line / per-region / per-branch numbers on the touched module *before* changing anything. After the fix, re-run the same command. If any number drops, find the missing test and add it before committing.
4. **Workspace conventions still apply.** Lint flags, derive lists, `no_std` constraints, doc comments on public items, spec-as-contract — all unchanged. Codex's suggestion is a finding, not a license to skip the project rules.
5. **Commit with a thread-linked message.** The commit message names the issue Codex raised, not just the file you touched. Example: `fix(group): clamp role on legacy adapters (Codex review #648)`.

### 6. Push, reply inline, resolve

After pushing:

**Reply** on each thread you addressed. Use the first comment's `databaseId` as the parent:

```bash
gh api -X POST repos/<owner>/<repo>/pulls/<pr>/comments \
  -F in_reply_to=<parent_databaseId> \
  -F body=$'Fixed in <commit-sha>: <one-line summary of the fix>'
```

**Resolve** the thread via GraphQL (uses the node id, not the database id):

```bash
gh api graphql -f query='
  mutation($threadId:ID!){
    resolveReviewThread(input:{threadId:$threadId}){ thread { isResolved } }
  }' -F threadId=<thread.id>
```

Only resolve threads where you actually landed a fix or replied with a justification the user has approved. Resolving without a real fix breaks the next reviewer's trust in the resolved-status signal.

### 7. Re-trigger Codex with `@codex review`

After every addressed thread has been replied to and resolved, post a fresh top-level comment on the PR with the literal body `@codex review`. This is required because Codex does **not** reliably re-trigger from a new push alone — sometimes the post-push hook fires, sometimes it doesn't, and waiting silently is indistinguishable from "Codex stalled". The explicit mention is the deterministic way to start the next pass.

```bash
gh pr comment <pr> --body "@codex review"
```

Only post this comment **after** the fix push and the thread replies/resolves have all landed — otherwise Codex starts the next pass against stale state and burns a full review cycle on outdated code.

If there's nothing to address but the loop is still waiting (e.g., 👀 was never added after a push), posting `@codex review` is also the right escalation before pinging the user.

### 8. Loop

Return to step 3 (snapshot) with refreshed `seen_*` sets. Codex's next pass picks up the comment within ~30–90 s, re-adds 👀, and either approves (👍) or surfaces new findings. Step 2 (initial `@codex review` trigger) is *not* repeated — step 7 already posted the trigger for the next pass.

## Polling cadence

Codex review runs typically complete in 1–5 minutes for a small PR, 10–20 for a large one. Pick the cadence by what you're waiting for:

- **First poll after push**: 60 seconds (Codex usually adds 👀 within 30–90 s).
- **Routine poll while 👀 is up**: 120 seconds (stays inside Anthropic's 5-min prompt cache window).
- **Routine poll while neither 👀 nor 👍 is present and no threads exist**: 120 seconds — Codex hasn't started yet (or finished a previous pass and a new one is pending).
- **👀 dropped + threads exist**: don't poll — switch immediately to the thread-handling subroutine.
- **No reaction at all after 5 minutes from push**: ping the user — Codex may not be configured for this repo, or the install is stalled. Also re-confirm the push actually landed.
- **👀 has been up >15 minutes with no thread activity**: ping the user — Codex may have stalled mid-run.

For dynamic-paced `/loop` runs, use `ScheduleWakeup` with `delaySeconds=120` (cache-warm) until 👍 lands. Don't poll faster than 60 seconds — Codex doesn't react that quickly anyway, and the prompt cache cost dominates.

**Prefer Bash `run_in_background` over Monitor for this loop.** The verdict we
wait for is a single terminal state (👍 approval, or 👀-dropped with new
findings) — exactly the "one notification when the condition is true" shape
Bash `run_in_background` is built for. Each iteration of the loop has exactly
one terminal condition to watch, so one completion notification per loop pass
is what we want.

Monitor is designed for ongoing event streams (one notification per
state-change line) — useful when you want to know about every transition in
real time, but notification delivery is best-effort and intermediate events
can drop. Real-world example from this skill's own bootstrap session: the 👀
→ 👍 transition fired correctly inside the monitor and was written to the
script's stdout file, but the follow-up `[change]` and `[approval]`
notifications never reached the conversation. The model sat waiting for an
event that had already fired.

Use the script below with `run_in_background: true` (not Monitor). It loops
until either terminal state, then exits with the verdict written to its last
stdout line so a single `Read` on the task's output file at notification time
recovers the verdict.

```bash
prev=""
while true; do
  reactions=$(gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions \
    --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"' 2>/dev/null | sort)
  threads=$(gh api graphql -f query='query($o:String!,$r:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$o,name:$r){pullRequest(number:$n){
      reviewThreads(first:100){nodes{id isResolved comments(first:1){nodes{author{login}}}}}}}}' \
    -F o=<owner> -F r=<repo> -F n=<pr> \
    --jq '.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes
          | map(select(.isResolved==false
                       and (.comments.nodes[0].author.login | test("codex"; "i"))))
          | length' 2>/dev/null)

  codex_eyes=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:eyes$" || true)
  codex_thumb=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:\+1$" || true)

  cur="reactions=[$reactions] threads=$threads"
  [ "$cur" != "$prev" ] && echo "[change] $cur"
  prev=$cur

  if [ -n "$codex_thumb" ]; then
    echo "VERDICT=approval"
    exit 0
  fi
  if [ -z "$codex_eyes" ] && [ "$threads" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "VERDICT=needs_action threads=$threads"
    exit 0
  fi

  sleep 120
done
```

When this script completes via Bash `run_in_background`, the single completion
notification fires. Recover the verdict with one `Read` on the task's output
file — the last line is `VERDICT=approval` or `VERDICT=needs_action threads=N`.
If the file's last line is something else (script crashed or hit the bash
timeout), apply the defensive re-check below.

### Defensive re-check: don't trust silence as "still reviewing"

Whether you use Bash `run_in_background` or `Monitor`, the delivery of
notifications back to the conversation is best-effort. If the runtime drops
the completion or change event, the loop sits waiting indefinitely for a
signal that already fired. The script's stdout file is authoritative;
the notification stream is convenience.

The mitigation is cheap and load-bearing: **after any unusually long quiet
period — more than ~10 minutes of total wait once you've seen 👀, or more
than ~5 minutes since the most recent event — do a direct verification
before continuing to wait or escalating to the user.** Verification is two
steps, in this order:

1. `Read` the background task's stdout file (the output path returned when
   the task started). If it contains a terminal line (the script's exit
   line, or an `[approval]` / `[needs_action]` event from a Monitor script)
   you missed the notification — act on it now.
2. Re-fetch PR state directly:
   ```bash
   gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<pr>/reactions \
     --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"'
   ```
   If the output shows `:+1` (👍), or shows 👀 absent with unresolved Codex
   threads, the run completed and you missed the signal. Re-run the
   threads query and act on the verdict.

The general principle: **the script's stdout file is the source of truth
for detection; the notification stream is best-effort delivery.** Treat
the two as separate channels — the file is durable, the channel is lossy.

Codex is usually right but not always. When you read a thread and your read of the spec or code says Codex is wrong (or redundant, or stylistic-without-justification):

1. **Do not unilaterally dismiss it.** Stop and surface to the user with: what Codex says, what the spec / code says, your reasoning, and a recommendation (address as-is / push back / close-as-wontfix).
2. **The user picks.** Then follow through — if "push back", reply inline with the technical reasoning; if "close-as-wontfix", reply inline with the rationale before resolving; if "address", fall through to step 5 of the loop.
3. **Never reply inline arguing the point without checking with the user first.** Codex's threads are read by humans too — a wrong reply costs more than a one-minute clarification.

## Anti-patterns

- **Resolving threads without a real fix or explicit user disagreement.** Marking resolved is load-bearing — the next reviewer trusts it. An unaddressed finding hidden by a resolution is invisible until production.
- **Skipping the test** because "the fix is obvious." Obvious fixes regress just as often as subtle ones. The test exists to prove the fix held over time, not to prove the fix was needed today.
- **Replying with "fixed" only.** Quote the commit sha and a one-line summary so the thread is self-contained when read later.
- **Polling faster than 60 seconds.** Burns prompt cache for no benefit. Codex doesn't react that fast.
- **Continuing past a 👍 in case there are still threads.** 👍 means Codex is satisfied with the current state — pending threads are stale. Re-verify with the user before bulk-resolving.
- **Arguing with Codex inline without escalating.** A wrong reply is more expensive than a one-minute user clarification.
- **Hard-coding the bot login.** Different installs use different handles (`chatgpt-codex-connector`, `codex[bot]`, etc.). Match by case-insensitive substring instead.
- **Treating background-task notifications as a complete event log.** Whether you use Bash `run_in_background` or Monitor, the conversation-side notification stream is best-effort delivery — the script's stdout file is authoritative. After any quiet period of >5 minutes since the last event (or >10 minutes total since 👀 was seen), `Read` the task's output path *and* re-fetch reactions directly with `gh api .../reactions` before assuming "still reviewing." See "Defensive re-check" under Polling cadence.
- **Using Monitor when you only need a terminal verdict.** Monitor is designed for ongoing event streams (every state change becomes a notification). When the loop is waiting for a single terminal verdict — 👍 or new findings — Bash `run_in_background` is the right tool: one reliable completion notification, single `Read` of the output file to recover the verdict. Reach for Monitor only when you need real-time visibility into every intermediate state change.

## What this skill is not

- **A code-review skill.** Actual fixes come from project-specific implementation skills (`post-implementation-audit`, `test-driven-development`, `systematic-debugging`). This skill is the wrapper that decides *when* those skills fire and how the result is reported back to GitHub.
- **A merge skill.** 👍 doesn't auto-merge — the user owns the merge decision. Stop the loop on 👍, hand back, let them merge.
- **A way to delegate judgment back to Codex.** If you disagree with a finding, surface to the user. Don't argue with the bot.
Following the skill. PR #706 was just pushed — let me post the initial `@codex review` trigger, then start the polling loop.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve runtime color-space changes across prop syncs**

When a user switches the picker via `Event::ChangeColorSpace`, any later unrelated prop update that triggers `SetProps` (for example changing `swatches`, `dir`, `disabled`, or positioning) will execute this unconditional assignment and reset `ctx.color_space` back to the original prop value. That makes runtime color-space selection fragile and causes the UI to jump back to the initial space even though the color-space prop itself did not change; update this field only when `old.color_space != new.color_space` or separate the initial/default prop from runtime state.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6GJcbs
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:681
parent_dbid: 3334857518
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Allow controlled open sync while disabled**

Because `on_props_changed` represents a controlled `open` prop change as `Event::Open`/`Event::Close`, this disabled guard drops parent-driven open synchronization for disabled pickers. For example, a picker mounted with `disabled: true, open: Some(true)` stays `State::Open` when the parent updates `open` to `Some(false)`, leaving the controlled prop unable to close or reopen the component until disabled is cleared; let controlled open sync events through this guard or use a dedicated sync event.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

=== THREAD PRRT_kwDORp4enM6GJcbw
file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:689
parent_dbid: 3334857523
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Close explicitly from the dragging state**

`State::Dragging` is still an open popover state, but explicit close requests only match `State::Open`, so a parent-controlled `open: Some(false)` update, `Api::close()`, or Escape-close routed while a drag is active is ignored and the picker remains stuck in `Dragging` with close lifecycle effects such as `DetachClickOutside` never emitted. Keep suppressing interact-outside during pointer capture, but handle explicit close events from `Dragging` as a normal close.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

parent_dbid: 3334857526
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Prevent the trigger button from submitting forms**

When the trigger attrs are applied to a real `<button>` inside a form, omitting `type="button"` leaves the HTML default submit behavior in place, so clicking the color-picker trigger can submit the form instead of only toggling the popover. The sibling popover trigger sets `HtmlAttr::Type` to `button`; this trigger should do the same before wiring `aria-haspopup`/toggle behavior.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Remove disabled channel thumbs from tab order**

With `props.disabled = true`, the area thumb is removed from the tab order, but each channel slider thumb still emits `tabindex="0"` and no disabled state. Disabled pickers therefore expose focusable slider controls whose keyboard events are dropped by the machine, which is confusing for keyboard and assistive-technology users; mirror the disabled handling used by the area thumb here.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve runtime color-space changes across prop syncs**

When a user switches the picker via `Event::ChangeColorSpace`, any later unrelated prop update that triggers `SetProps` (for example changing `swatches`, `dir`, `disabled`, or positioning) will execute this unconditional assignment and reset `ctx.color_space` back to the original prop value. That makes runtime color-space selection fragile and causes the UI to jump back to the initial space even though the color-space prop itself did not change; update this field only when `old.color_space != new.color_space` or separate the initial/default prop from runtime state.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Allow controlled open sync while disabled**

Because `on_props_changed` represents a controlled `open` prop change as `Event::Open`/`Event::Close`, this disabled guard drops parent-driven open synchronization for disabled pickers. For example, a picker mounted with `disabled: true, open: Some(true)` stays `State::Open` when the parent updates `open` to `Some(false)`, leaving the controlled prop unable to close or reopen the component until disabled is cleared; let controlled open sync events through this guard or use a dedicated sync event.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Close explicitly from the dragging state**

`State::Dragging` is still an open popover state, but explicit close requests only match `State::Open`, so a parent-controlled `open: Some(false)` update, `Api::close()`, or Escape-close routed while a drag is active is ignored and the picker remains stuck in `Dragging` with close lifecycle effects such as `DetachClickOutside` never emitted. Keep suppressing interact-outside during pointer capture, but handle explicit close events from `Dragging` as a normal close.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Prevent the trigger button from submitting forms**

When the trigger attrs are applied to a real `<button>` inside a form, omitting `type="button"` leaves the HTML default submit behavior in place, so clicking the color-picker trigger can submit the form instead of only toggling the popover. The sibling popover trigger sets `HtmlAttr::Type` to `button`; this trigger should do the same before wiring `aria-haspopup`/toggle behavior.

fn context_relevant_props_changed(old: &Props, new: &Props) -> bool {
    old.disabled != new.disabled
        || old.readonly != new.readonly
        || old.close_on_interact_outside != new.close_on_interact_outside
        || old.close_on_escape != new.close_on_escape
        || old.show_alpha != new.show_alpha
        || old.color_space != new.color_space
        || old.swatches != new.swatches
        || old.dir != new.dir
        || old.positioning != new.positioning
        || (old.channel_step - new.channel_step).abs() > f64::EPSILON
        || (old.channel_large_step - new.channel_large_step).abs() > f64::EPSILON
}

content.text: Now realign both edited tables (the §2 anatomy table and the §3.1 ARIA table).

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
python3 - <<'PY'
path="spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md"
lines=open(path).read().split("\n")

def realign(header_pred):
    start=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if header_pred(l))
    end=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines[start:], start) if l.strip()=="")
    rows=[]
    for l in lines[start:end]:
        if set(l.strip())<=set("|-: "): rows.append(None); continue
        rows.append([c.strip() for c in l.strip().strip("|").split("|")])
    ncol=len(next(r for r in rows if r))
    widths=[0]*ncol
    for r in rows:
        if r:
            for i,c in enumerate(r): widths[i]=max(widths[i],len(c))
    def fmt(r): return "| "+" | ".join(c.ljust(widths[i]) for i,c in enumerate(r))+" |"
    def sep(): return "| "+" | ".join("-"*widths[i] for i in range(ncol))+" |"
    lines[start:end]=[sep() if r is None else fmt(r) for r in rows]
    return start+1,end

# Anatomy table (§2): header "| Part ... Key Attributes"
a=realign(lambda l: l.startswith("| Part ") and "Key Attributes" in l)
# ARIA table (§3.1): header "| Attribute / Behaviour"
b=realign(lambda l: l.startswith("| Attribute / Behaviour"))
open(path,"w").write("\n".join(lines))
print("realigned anatomy", a, "and aria", b)
PY

content.text:
I've addressed all 5 Codex findings and am waiting on the coverage gate before pushing. Summary of the fixes in flight:

1. **Runtime color-space preservation** — `color_space` is now driven by `ChangeColorSpace` (a prop change routes through it in `on_props_changed`) and removed from the blanket `SetProps` sync, so an unrelated prop update no longer reverts a runtime switch.
2. **Controlled open sync while disabled** — the disabled guard now lets `Open`/`Close` through (only user `Toggle`/interaction is ignored).
3. **Close from `Dragging`** — `Close`/`Toggle`/`CloseOnEscape` now resolve from `Dragging` (abandoning the drag), while interact-outside stays suppressed during pointer capture.
4. **Trigger `type="button"`** — prevents form submission.
5. **Disabled channel thumbs + swatches** — now `tabindex="-1"` + `aria-disabled` like the area thumb.

Each has a new test (TDD), two existing disabled-semantics tests were updated, the 3 trigger snapshots re-accepted (only `type="button"` added), and the spec §1.7/§1.8/§2/§3.1 synced. Tests (85), clippy `-D warnings`, and the insta orphan check are all green; spec validates and snippets compile.

Once `cargo xcov` confirms the branch threshold holds, I'll commit, push, reply+resolve each Codex thread, and re-trigger `@codex review` — looping until 👍.

attachment.content:
=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
git add -A
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(color-picker): address Codex review #706 lifecycle/prop-sync/a11y findings

- Preserve runtime color-space switches across prop syncs: color_space is now
  owned by ChangeColorSpace (a prop change routes through it in
  on_props_changed) and dropped from the blanket SetProps sync and
  context_relevant_props_changed, so an unrelated prop update no longer reverts
  a runtime switch.
- Honor controlled open sync while disabled: the disabled guard lets Open/Close
  through (only user-initiated Toggle/interaction stays blocked), so a disabled
  controlled picker still reflects parent-driven open changes.
- Resolve explicit close from the Dragging state: Close/Toggle/CloseOnEscape and
  a parent-controlled open:false now abandon the drag and close (emitting the
  close lifecycle effects) instead of getting stuck; interact-outside stays
  suppressed during pointer capture.
- Set type="button" on the trigger so a real <button> in a form toggles the
  popover instead of submitting (mirrors the popover trigger).
- Remove disabled channel slider thumbs and swatches from the tab order
  (tabindex="-1" + aria-disabled), mirroring the area thumb.

Adds named tests for each fix; updates the two disabled-semantics tests and the
three trigger snapshots (type="button"); syncs spec sections 1.7/1.8/2/3.1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1
git push 2>&1 | tail -4

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
prev=""
while true; do
  reactions=$(gh api repos/fogodev/ars-ui/issues/706/reactions --jq '.[] | "\(.user.login):\(.content)"' 2>/dev/null | sort)
  threads=$(gh api graphql -f query='query($o:String!,$r:String!,$n:Int!){
    repository(owner:$o,name:$r){pullRequest(number:$n){
      reviewThreads(first:100){nodes{id isResolved comments(first:1){nodes{author{login}}}}}}}}' \
    -F o=fogodev -F r=ars-ui -F n=706 \
    --jq '.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes
          | map(select(.isResolved==false and (.comments.nodes[0].author.login | test("codex"; "i")))) | length' 2>/dev/null)
  codex_eyes=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:eyes$" || true)
  codex_thumb=$(echo "$reactions" | grep -E "codex.*:\+1$" || true)
  cur="reactions=[$reactions] threads=$threads"
  [ "$cur" != "$prev" ] && echo "[change] $cur"
  prev=$cur
  if [ -n "$codex_thumb" ]; then echo "VERDICT=approval"; exit 0; fi
  if [ -z "$codex_eyes" ] && [ "${threads:-0}" -gt 0 ]; then echo "VERDICT=needs_action threads=$threads"; exit 0; fi
  sleep 120
done

If a parent disables the picker while the user is dragging, `SetProps` can put the context in `disabled = true` while the state remains `Dragging`; the subsequent pointer-up `DragEnd` is then rejected by this guard, so the machine stays in `Dragging` and never fires `on_change_end` or clears `data-ars-dragging`. The sibling `ColorArea` explicitly allows `DragEnd` through this disabled guard for the same mid-drag-disable scenario, so this component should do the same.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:789  parent_dbid: 3335042506
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Remap controls when color space changes**

For `Event::ChangeColorSpace`, this only updates `ctx.color_space`, but the actual editable controls still hard-code the area to saturation/lightness and the non-alpha slider to hue. When `Props::color_space` starts as or changes to `Rgb`/`Hsb`, `Context::channels()` advertises a different channel set, yet area drags/keyboard and slider labels continue editing HSL channels, so the visible controls do not match the selected color space.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:537  parent_dbid: 3335042510
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve hue endpoint on channel drags**

When the hue slider is dragged to `x == 1.0` or set to its max via keyboard, this sends `360` through `with_channel`, which constructs a `ColorValue` whose hue is wrapped into `[0, 360)`. The value immediately becomes `0`, so the thumb position and `aria-valuenow` jump back to the start instead of staying at the right-hand 360° endpoint; the standalone `ColorSlider` keeps an unwrapped slider value specifically to avoid this endpoint collapse.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:1355  parent_dbid: 3335042519
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Mark the disabled area thumb for assistive tech**

In the disabled picker case, the area thumb only leaves the tab order; unlike the channel thumbs and swatches, it never receives `aria-disabled="true"`. Because this custom `role="application"` control is still present in the accessibility tree, screen-reader users can encounter it without any disabled state even though the machine drops its editing events.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:310  parent_dbid: 3335042524
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Include alpha in the channel list when enabled**

`channels()` is documented as the adapter source for laying out per-channel inputs, but every returned slice omits `ColorChannel::Alpha` even though `show_alpha` defaults to true and the props promise an alpha slider and input. An adapter that follows this API will render RGB/HSL/HSB inputs but never render the alpha input, leaving translucent colors impossible to edit numerically.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:1110  parent_dbid: 3335042528
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Suppress HSL alpha when alpha controls are hidden**

For a translucent value with `show_alpha = false`, the Hex and RGB branches intentionally hide alpha, but the HSL branch calls `to_css_hsl()`, which emits `hsla(...)` whenever `alpha < 1.0`. Switching the visible text format to HSL therefore exposes an alpha value even though the alpha controls and submitted hex value are configured to hide it.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:918  parent_dbid: 3335042539
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Sync the open bindable when control is removed**

When a controlled picker changes from `open: Some(...)` back to `open: None`, this path emits no event, and `SetProps` also ignores `open`, so `ctx.open` keeps the stale controlled slot forever. After that, user toggles only mutate the internal value while `ctx.open.get()` still returns the old controlled value, making the public context disagree with the actual `State`.

file: crates/ars-components/src/specialized/color_picker/mod.rs:1527  parent_dbid: 3335042543
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Mark preset swatches as non-submit buttons**

When an adapter renders swatches as native `<button>` elements inside a form, these attrs do not provide `type="button"`, so selecting a preset uses the browser's default submit behavior instead of only updating the color. The trigger already sets `type="button"` for this reason; the picker’s other action buttons should do the same.

content.text:
Codex's second pass found **8 P2 findings**. Seven are clear bugs I'll fix (DragEnd-while-disabled, hue-endpoint collapse on the hue slider, area-thumb `aria-disabled`, `channels()` omitting alpha, HSL leaking alpha when `show_alpha=false`, the stale controlled-`open` slot, and swatch `type="button"`). One is a genuine design fork with a spec contradiction that I should not resolve unilaterally.

Let me verify the spec contradiction before asking.

echo "=== DragTarget::Area (says fixed) ==="; grep -n "fixed in ColorPicker\|xChannel=Saturation" spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md

content.content:
=== §1.6 (says remap) ===
### 1.6 Color Space Switching

`ColorPicker` supports runtime color space switching via `Event::ChangeColorSpace(ColorSpace)`.

**Transition Behavior** for `ChangeColorSpace(new_space)`:

1. **Recompute value**: Convert the current color value from the old color space to `new_space`. Preserve perceptual magnitudes where possible (e.g., HSL->HSB preserves hue and saturation, converts lightness to brightness).
2. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. Update `ctx.value` with the converted color.
3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.
=== DragTarget::Area (says fixed) ===
400:    /// 2D area: xChannel=Saturation, yChannel=Lightness (fixed in ColorPicker).
=== anatomy Area description ===
1451:│   ├── Area                    (required — 2D saturation/lightness gradient)

3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.

content.text: Confirmed: the spec contradicts itself — §1.6 step 3 says the area/sliders remap channels per color space ("RGB→HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation"), while `DragTarget::Area` and the anatomy declare the area fixed to saturation/lightness. Codex's BN finding rests on §1.6. This is a design decision I shouldn't make unilaterally.

questions.question: Codex flagged that the 2D area + hue slider don't remap their channels when the color space changes. The spec is self-contradictory here: §1.6 step 3 says the area/sliders should remap ("RGB→HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation"), but `DragTarget::Area` and the §2 anatomy declare the area fixed to saturation/lightness. How should ColorPicker treat color space vs. the 2D area? (The other 7 Codex findings are unambiguous bugs I'm fixing regardless.)

options.label: Fixed area, fix spec §1.6
options.description: Keep the 2D area as a fixed saturation×lightness square (saturation×brightness for HSB) of the current hue, with the hue + alpha sliders fixed. color_space drives only the numeric channel inputs + format display. This is the standard Ark UI / React Aria picker UX; RGB has no natural hue-area decomposition. I'd rewrite the contradictory §1.6 step 3 to match, and reply to Codex with this rationale. (Recommended)

options.label: Full per-space area remap
options.description: Make the area's x/y channels and the non-alpha slider channel derive from color_space (incl. an RGB decomposition). Substantial design+impl work (effectively a 13-pt expansion) touching apply_drag_position, area/slider attrs, channels(), and drag/keyboard; the RGB area mapping has no obvious standard. Larger PR scope.

options.label: HSB/HSL axis only
options.description: Keep the area as the visual square but switch its y-axis between lightness (HSL/RGB/HEX) and brightness (HSB) based on color_space; keep hue slider fixed; color_space still drives numeric inputs. Middle ground: the area tracks the perceptual space without attempting an RGB area. I'd reconcile §1.6 to this narrower behavior.

answers.Codex flagged that the 2D area + hue sli: What are the tradeoffs of each approach? There is a clear winner that will give our library the best outcome possible?

content.text:
The user wants the tradeoffs and the clear winner. Here's my analysis:

**Option 2 (full per-space remap)** is the one §1.6's example literally describes — but no real picker implements it. RGB has no perceptually-navigable 2D "hue square," so a "red×green area with a blue slider" is meaningless to users; Ark UI and React Aria both render RGB as plain channel sliders/inputs, never a 2D area. It's the most work *and* a worse UX. Reject.

**Option 1 (always saturation×lightness)** is simplest and matches the `DragTarget`/anatomy text, but it's subtly wrong in HSB mode: the visual square stays sat×*lightness* while the numeric inputs show *brightness*, so the picker isn't truly HSB-correct despite advertising first-class HSB support.

**Option 3 (area = saturation × {lightness for HSL/RGB/HEX, brightness for HSB}; hue+alpha sliders fixed; color_space drives the numeric inputs)** is exactly how real pickers behave — the 2D square reflects the chosen cylindrical space's S and L/V axes (the classic HSV square vs HSL square), hue/alpha are always hue/alpha, and RGB/HEX reuse the HSL square as the visual picker since they have no hue square. It's bounded (parametrize one axis), HSB-correct, and lets me reconcile §1.6 to a coherent contract instead of its nonsensical RGB example.

**Clear winner: Option 3.** It's the best-outcome design without Option 2's invented RGB area. I'll implement it plus the 7 bug fixes. Let me track this batch and start.

lines: /// The current color value (controlled or uncontrolled).
-    /// Whether the picker popover is open (controlled or uncontrolled).
+    /// Whether the picker popover is open.
+    /// Mirrors [`State`] (which is the source of truth for open/closed). A plain
+    /// distinction is resolved by `on_props_changed` emitting `Open`/`Close`, so
+    /// there is no separate controlled slot to drift out of sync with `State`.
+    /// The unwrapped hue in degrees `[0, 360]` for the hue channel slider.
+    /// [`ColorValue`] normalizes hue into `[0, 360)`, so reading hue back from
+    /// the color collapses the 360° endpoint onto 0°. Tracking it here keeps the
+    /// hue slider's thumb position and `aria-valuenow` at the right-hand
+    /// endpoint, exactly as the standalone `ColorSlider` does.

895	
896	`ColorPicker` supports runtime color space switching via `Event::ChangeColorSpace(ColorSpace)`.
897	
898	**Transition Behavior** for `ChangeColorSpace(new_space)`:
899	
900	1. **Recompute value**: Convert the current color value from the old color space to `new_space`. Preserve perceptual magnitudes where possible (e.g., HSL->HSB preserves hue and saturation, converts lightness to brightness).
901	2. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. Update `ctx.value` with the converted color.
902	3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
903	4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.
904	
905	**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.
906	
907	**Supported Spaces**: All spaces in the `ColorSpace` enum (RGB, HSL, HSB/HSV, HEX) are switchable at runtime. The conversion between spaces is lossless for the sRGB gamut (minor floating-point rounding is acceptable).

file.content:
`ColorPicker` supports runtime color space switching via `Event::ChangeColorSpace(ColorSpace)`.

**Transition Behavior** for `ChangeColorSpace(new_space)`:

1. **Recompute value**: Convert the current color value from the old color space to `new_space`. Preserve perceptual magnitudes where possible (e.g., HSL->HSB preserves hue and saturation, converts lightness to brightness).
2. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. Update `ctx.value` with the converted color.
3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.

**Supported Spaces**: All spaces in the `ColorSpace` enum (RGB, HSL, HSB/HSV, HEX) are switchable at runtime. The conversion between spaces is lossless for the sRGB gamut (minor floating-point rounding is acceptable).

### 1.7 Full Machine Implementation

input.old_string:
**Transition Behavior** for `ChangeColorSpace(new_space)`:

1. **Recompute value**: Convert the current color value from the old color space to `new_space`. Preserve perceptual magnitudes where possible (e.g., HSL->HSB preserves hue and saturation, converts lightness to brightness).
2. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. Update `ctx.value` with the converted color.
3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.
input.new_string:
**Transition Behavior** for `ChangeColorSpace(new_space)`:

1. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. The stored `ColorValue` is unchanged — the same color is simply presented through the new space's parameters.
2. **Remap the numeric inputs**: `Context::channels()` returns the new space's channel set (RGB→`[R, G, B]`, HSL→`[H, S, L]`, HSB→`[H, S, B]`, plus `Alpha` when `show_alpha`), so the per-channel text inputs re-label and re-bind to the new channels.
3. **Remap the 2D area's vertical axis**: the area is always the visual saturation × {lightness | brightness} square of the current hue — `Context::area_y_channel()` returns brightness in HSB and lightness in HSL/RGB/HEX. The horizontal axis is always saturation, and the hue and alpha sliders are space-independent (always hue/alpha). The area is **not** remapped to arbitrary channel pairs: RGB/HEX have no perceptually-navigable hue square (they are edited through the numeric inputs), so they reuse the HSL square as the visual picker. This is the standard Ark UI / React Aria picker model.
4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

A controlled `color_space` prop change is routed through this same `Event::ChangeColorSpace` from `on_props_changed`, so a prop-driven switch announces and remaps identically to a runtime one — and `SetProps` never touches `color_space`, so an unrelated prop update cannot revert a runtime switch.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.

-2. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. Update `ctx.value` with the converted color.

-3. **Remap channels**: The color area and channel sliders update to reflect the new space's channels (e.g., switching from RGB to HSL changes the area from Red/Green to Hue/Saturation, and the slider from Blue to Lightness).
+1. **Update context**: Set `ctx.color_space = new_space`. The stored `ColorValue` is unchanged — the same color is simply presented through the new space's parameters.

+2. **Remap the numeric inputs**: `Context::channels()` returns the new space's channel set (RGB→`[R, G, B]`, HSL→`[H, S, L]`, HSB→`[H, S, B]`, plus `Alpha` when `show_alpha`), so the per-channel text inputs re-label and re-bind to the new channels.

+3. **Remap the 2D area's vertical axis**: the area is always the visual saturation × {lightness | brightness} square of the current hue — `Context::area_y_channel()` returns brightness in HSB and lightness in HSL/RGB/HEX. The horizontal axis is always saturation, and the hue and alpha sliders are space-independent (always hue/alpha). The area is **not** remapped to arbitrary channel pairs: RGB/HEX have no perceptually-navigable hue square (they are edited through the numeric inputs), so they reuse the HSL square as the visual picker. This is the standard Ark UI / React Aria picker model.

4. **Live region announcement**: Emit an `aria-live="polite"` announcement: `"Switched to {space} color space"` (e.g., "Switched to HSL color space"). This informs screen reader users that the control layout has changed.

+A controlled `color_space` prop change is routed through this same `Event::ChangeColorSpace` from `on_props_changed`, so a prop-driven switch announces and remaps identically to a runtime one — and `SetProps` never touches `color_space`, so an unrelated prop update cannot revert a runtime switch.

**UI Affordance**: The color space selector (button group, dropdown, or segmented control) emits `Event::ChangeColorSpace(selected_space)` when the user selects a different space. The spec does not prescribe a specific UI pattern -- adapters choose the appropriate control.

**Supported Spaces**: All spaces in the `ColorSpace` enum (RGB, HSL, HSB/HSV, HEX) are switchable at runtime. The conversion between spaces is lossless for the sRGB gamut (minor floating-point rounding is acceptable).

  `max`, `aria-label`, the thumb-position custom property, and `data-ars-dragging`
  for the matching channel; `tabindex="-1"` + `aria-disabled` when disabled. The
  hue thumb reports the unwrapped `Context::hue_value` so it stays at the 360°
  endpoint instead of wrapping to 0°.
- **`swatch_attrs(index)`** — `type="button"`, `role="button"`, `data-ars-index`,
  `tabindex` (`-1` + `aria-disabled` when disabled), and (when `index` is in
  range) `aria-label` from `messages.swatch_label`, the `--ars-swatch-color`
  custom property, and `data-ars-selected` when the swatch equals the current
  value. An out-of-range index yields the base attributes only.

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
python3 - <<'PY'
path="spec/components/specialized/color-picker.md"
lines=open(path).read().split("\n")
start=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if l.startswith("| Part ") and "Key Attributes" in l)
end=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines[start:], start) if l.strip()=="")
rows=[]
for l in lines[start:end]:
    if set(l.strip())<=set("|-: "): rows.append(None); continue
    rows.append([c.strip() for c in l.strip().strip("|").split("|")])
ncol=len(next(r for r in rows if r)); widths=[0]*ncol
for r in rows:
    if r:
        for i,c in enumerate(r): widths[i]=max(widths[i],len(c))
def fmt(r): return "| "+" | ".join(c.ljust(widths[i]) for i,c in enumerate(r))+" |"
def sep(): return "| "+" | ".join("-"*widths[i] for i in range(ncol))+" |"
lines[start:end]=[sep() if r is None else fmt(r) for r in rows]
open(path,"w").write("\n".join(lines))
print("anatomy table realigned", start+1, end)
PY

attachment.content:
=== RUST SKILLS DISPLAY FORMAT ===
When showing Rust Skills loaded, display in this EXACT order:
1. FIRST: "🦀 Rust Skills Loaded" text
2. THEN: The Ferris crab ASCII art BELOW the text
The text must be ABOVE the crab, not below.
===

=== MANDATORY: META-COGNITION ROUTING ===

CRITICAL: You MUST follow the COMPLETE meta-cognition framework.
Partial compliance (only loading L1 skill) is NOT acceptable.

## STEP 1: IDENTIFY ENTRY LAYER + DOMAIN

### Layer 1 Signals (Start here, trace UP):
- Error codes: E0382, E0597, E0277, E0499, etc.
- Keywords: cannot be sent, moved value, borrowed, lifetime

### Layer 3 Domain Signals (MUST also load domain skill):

| Keywords in Question | Domain Skill to Load |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Web API, HTTP, REST, axum, actix, handler, router | domain-web |
| payment, trading, fintech, decimal, currency | domain-fintech |
| CLI, command line, clap, terminal | domain-cli |
| embedded, no_std, MCU, firmware | domain-embedded |
| kubernetes, docker, grpc, microservice | domain-cloud-native |
| MQTT, sensor, IoT, telemetry | domain-iot |
| tensor, model, inference, ML | domain-ml |

**CRITICAL**: If domain keywords are present, you MUST load BOTH:
1. The Layer 1 skill (e.g., m07-concurrency)
2. The Layer 3 domain skill (e.g., domain-web)

## STEP 2: EXECUTE TRACING (MANDATORY)

For Layer 1 entry with domain context:

```
L1 Error (e.g., Rc not Send)
    ^ TRACE UP: Why this design constraint?
    ^ Load domain skill (e.g., domain-web)
    ^ Find domain constraint (e.g., "handlers run on any thread")
    v TRACE DOWN: What pattern satisfies constraint?
    v Return to L1 with context-aware solution
```

## STEP 3: MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT

Your response MUST include ALL of these sections:

### Reasoning Chain
```
+-- Layer 1: [specific error]
|   Problem: [error description]
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: [domain name] (domain-xxx)
|   Constraint: [what is the domain constraint]
|   Rule: [why this constraint exists]
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: [design decision based on domain constraint]
```

### Domain Constraints Analysis
- MUST reference specific rules from domain-xxx skill
- Explain WHY this domain has this constraint

### Recommended Solution
- Provide solution that follows domain best practices
- Not just fixing the compile error

## EXAMPLE: Web API + Send Error

Question: "Web API config sharing error: Rc cannot be sent"

CORRECT Response:
```
### Reasoning Chain
+-- Layer 1: Send/Sync Error
|   Problem: Rc<T> cannot be sent between threads
|       ^
+-- Layer 3: Web Domain (domain-web)
|   Constraint: Handlers run on any thread
|   Rule: Shared state must be thread-safe
|       v
+-- Layer 2: Design Choice
    Decision: Use Arc<T> + State extractor

### Domain Constraints Analysis
From domain-web:
- "Rc in state" is a Common Mistake
- Web handlers require Send + Sync
- Recommended: axum State<Arc<T>> pattern

### Recommended Solution
[Code following Web best practices]
```

WRONG Response (stops at L1):
```
Problem: Rc is not Send
Solution: Use Arc
```

## SKILLS TO INVOKE

Always invoke with Skill() tool:
- Skill(rust-router) - First, to get routing
- Skill(m0x-xxx) - Layer 1 skill based on error
- Skill(domain-xxx) - Layer 3 skill based on domain keywords

===================================

input.command:
cd /Users/ericson/Workspace/Rust/ars-ui.worktrees/impl-300
git add -A
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(color-picker): address Codex review #706 second-pass findings

- Terminate drags after a mid-drag disable: DragEnd passes the disabled guard so
  the gesture ends cleanly (fires on_change_end, clears data-ars-dragging),
  matching ColorArea.
- Make the 2D area color-space-aware (reconciles the spec's own §1.6 vs
  DragTarget contradiction): the area edits saturation × {lightness | brightness}
  via Context::area_y_channel() (brightness in HSB), with the hue/alpha sliders
  space-independent. RGB/HEX reuse the HSL square (no perceptual hue square) and
  are edited via the numeric inputs. color_space drives the numeric channel set.
- Preserve the hue 360° endpoint: track an unwrapped Context::hue_value for the
  hue slider/keyboard so dragging or stepping to the max no longer collapses to
  0° (ColorValue wraps hue into [0,360)).
- Mark the disabled area thumb with aria-disabled (it was only leaving the tab
  order), matching the channel thumbs and swatches.
- Include Alpha in Context::channels() when show_alpha, so adapters render the
  promised alpha input.
- Suppress alpha in the HSL text branch when show_alpha is false (it emitted
  hsla() via to_css_hsl()), matching the Hex/RGB branches and the hidden value.
- Sync the open state as a plain bool instead of Bindable<bool>, eliminating the
  stale controlled-slot divergence with State when a controlled open prop is
  removed.
- Set type="button" on preset swatches so a swatch rendered as a native <button>
  in a form selects the color instead of submitting (mirrors the trigger).

Adds named tests for each; updates the channels/disabled tests and the swatch
snapshots; adds Messages::brightness_label; syncs spec §1.3/1.5/1.6/1.7/1.8/2/3.1/4.1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1
git push 2>&1 | tail -3
